title: 3. Keep Your Distance | The Hollywood Hack
format: The third episode of Brian Raftery's 'The Hollywood Hack' follows 'The Interview' from pitch to near-cancellation. Raftery traces how North Korea became Hollywood's default villain, from Team America to a digitally re-nationalized Red Dawn to Olympus Has Fallen, before Seth Rogen and Evan Goldberg put Kim Jong-un's head in a slow-motion fireball. Screenwriter Dan Sterling and Belcourt Theater programmer Toby Leonard walk through the leaked Amy Pascal emails over the death scene, the chains pulling the film after a 9/11 threat, and the Christmas Day release that became a patriotic event. It ends on the hack's long tail: Pascal's exit and the Hollywood pay-gap reckoning.

segments.heading: ‘The Interview’
segments.note: the movie that supposedly started the mess

films.title: The Interview
films.note: Seth Rogen and Evan Goldberg's comedy, with Rogen and James Franco as journalists the CIA recruits to kill Kim Jong-un (Randall Park), whose head is blown off in a slow-motion explosion at the climax. Screenwriter Dan Sterling started from a Rogen-Goldberg 'what if you interviewed Osama bin Laden and had to kill him' provocation, then moved the target to North Korea over drinks with a friend. His first draft used a fictional leader named Kim Il-Hwan; the real name came later, at the studio's suggestion. Sony CEO Kazuo Hirai pushed to soften the death scene, kicking off months of emails between Amy Pascal and Rogen ('maybe it's more charred than pink') that ended with the kill scored to an acoustic version of Katy Perry's 'Firework.' After a threat invoking 9/11, the four biggest chains pulled it; Sony released it Christmas Day 2014 in more than 300 indie theaters and same-day on Google, the first big studio film to open in theaters and streaming at once. It made about $12 million worldwide, and no violence followed.

films.title: Long Shot
films.note: The Dan Sterling screenplay (a romance between a journalist and a US politician) he'd just finished when he met Rogen and Goldberg. It took years to reach the screen.

films.title: Zero Dark Thirty
films.note: Sony's bin Laden thriller, cited as proof the studio would defend a troublesome movie. Senators including John McCain and Dianne Feinstein wrote CEO Michael Linton demanding a correction over its torture scenes; Sony refused to change the film.

films.title: Team America: World Police
films.note: Matt Stone and Trey Parker's puppet musical, the highest-profile takedown of Kim Jong-il, who dies impaled on a German helmet. In 2005 North Korea asked the Czech Republic, where it had an embassy, to ban it. The request was denied, the only official objection Raftery could find before 'The Interview.'

films.title: Die Another Day
films.note: Pierce Brosnan's James Bond faces off against heavily armed North Koreans, released the same year Bush named North Korea to the 'axis of evil.'

films.title: Behind Enemy Lines II: Axis of Evil
films.note: The direct-to-video military drama Raftery jokes 'sounds like something Dick Cheney would watch on his Xbox.'

films.title: Red Dawn
films.note: MGM's remake of the 1984 hit, shot with Chinese invaders, then digitally changed in post to North Koreans so the film wouldn't alienate the Chinese box office.

films.title: Olympus Has Fallen
films.note: North Korean forces storm the White House and execute the vice president.

segments.heading: Killing a real leader — the comedy precedent
segments.note: it had been done cartoonishly before

films.title: Hot Shots! Part Deux
films.note: One of the 90s comedies that assassinated Saddam Hussein as a gag, cited as a cartoonish precedent for putting a real leader's death on screen.

films.title: South Park: Bigger, Longer & Uncut
films.note: The South Park movie, also named for killing off Saddam Hussein.

films.title: The Naked Gun
films.note: Frank Drebin roughs up several US adversaries, including Ayatollah Khomeini and Muammar Gaddafi, a slapstick version of the taboo 'The Interview' would take literally.

referenced.group: The risky studio movies Raftery misses
